nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of lagoon and seaward reefs, generally to about 15 m depth

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting frozen, freeze-dried, fresh, and flake foods in captivity. A varied diet including zooplankton, small crustaceans such as mysis and artemia, and regular vegetable-based foods is generally advised. Vitamin supplements are recommended. Newly added individuals may initially refuse food and tend to benefit from live foods or frequent small feedings to encourage acceptance.

avoidWith: other Bannerfish species unless introduced simultaneously

summary: The Phantom Bannerfish is a boldly patterned Indian Ocean butterflyfish, recognized by its broad black and white bands, yellow accents, and distinctive forehead horns. Generally considered peaceful, it is most often kept in non-reef aquariums, as its tendency to nip at tubeworms and clams makes reef compatibility uncertain.
careNotes:
The Phantom Bannerfish is generally considered shy when first introduced and tends to benefit from plenty of hiding places in the rockwork. Over time, individuals typically become more bold and comfortable in the aquarium. It can be kept singly, in pairs, or in small groups in larger tanks, though territorial behavior has been reported when housed with other Bannerfish species; simultaneous introduction is generally advised to reduce aggression. This species tends to eat tubeworms and has been reported to nibble at clams (including Tridacna spp.), so reef tank compatibility should be considered carefully.

Temperature note: Aquarium care guides consistently advise a tank temperature of 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), while recorded wild habitat temperatures in the Indian Ocean run warmer at 28.1–29.4°C. These figures reflect different things — the ocean surface conditions of its natural range versus the commonly recommended captive range.

Breeding in home aquariums has not been well documented.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous in the wild, forming pairs during the breeding season. Captive breeding has not been well documented and is generally considered very difficult to achieve in home aquariums.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; forms pairs during breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.
